content:
I am second-year PhD student in Computer Science advised by Prof. Alessandra Russo and Dr. Krysia Broda at Imperial College London. Previously, I worked as research assistant at Universitat Pompeu Fabra under the supervision of Dr. Anders Jonsson. The focus of my research was on planning formalisms involving concurrent actions, mainly multiagent planning and temporal planning.
I hold a MSc in Intelligent Interactive Systems and a BSc in Computer Engineering from Universitat Pompeu Fabra in Barcelona.
summary:
I am second-year PhD student in Computer Science advised by Prof. Alessandra Russo and Dr. Krysia Broda at Imperial College London. Previously, I worked as research assistant at Universitat Pompeu Fabra under the supervision of Dr. Anders Jonsson. The focus of my research was on planning formalisms involving concurrent actions, mainly multiagent planning and temporal planning.
I hold a MSc in Intelligent Interactive Systems and a BSc in Computer Engineering from Universitat Pompeu Fabra in Barcelona.
title: Daniel Furelos-Blanco

summary: In this work we present a novel approach to solving concurrent multiagent planning problems in which several agents act in parallel. Our approach relies on a compilation from concurrent multiagent planning to classical planning, allowing us to use an off-the-shelf classical planner to solve the original multiagent problem. The solution can be directly interpreted as a concurrent plan that satisfies a given set of concurrency constraints, while avoiding the exponential blowup associated with  concurrent  actions.  Our  planner  is  the  first  to  handle action effects that are conditional on what other agents are doing. Theoretically, we show that the compilation is sound and complete. Empirically, we show that our compilation can solve challenging multiagent planning problems that require concurrent actions.
title: Solving Multiagent Planning Problems with Concurrent Conditional Effects

summary: In this paper we present the CARPooL demonstrator, an implementation of a Collective Adaptation Engine (CAE) that addresses the challenge of collective adaptation in the smart mobility domain. CARPooL resolves adaptation issues via concurrent planning techniques. It also allows to interact with the provided solutions by adding new issues or analyzing the actions done by each agent.
title: CARPooL: Collective Adaptation using concuRrent PLanning

summary: In this paper we address the challenges that impede collective adaptation in smart mobility systems by proposing a notion of ensembles. Ensembles enable systems with collective adaptability to be built as emergent aggregations of autonomous and self-adaptive agents. Adaptation in these systems is triggered by a run-time occurrence, which is known as an issue. The novel aspect of our approach is, it allows agents affected by an issue in the context of a smart mobility scenario to adapt collaboratively with minimal impact on their own preferences through an issue resolution process based on concurrent planning algorithms.
title: Collective Adaptation through Concurrent Planning: the Case of Sustainable Urban Mobility

summary: In this paper we describe STP, a novel algorithm for temporal planning. Similar to several existing temporal planners, STP relies on a transformation from temporal planning to classical planning, and constructs a temporal plan by finding a sequence of classical actions that solve the problem while satisfying a given set of temporal constraints. Our main contribution is that STP can solve temporal planning problems that require simultaneous events, i.e. the temporal actions have to be scheduled in such a way that two or more of their effects take place concurrently. To do so, STP separates each event into three phases: one phase in which temporal actions are scheduled to end, one phase in which simultaneous effects take place, and one phase in which temporal actions are scheduled to start. Experimental results show that STP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art temporal planners in a domain requiring simultaneous events.
title: Forward-Search Temporal Planning with Simultaneous Events

summary: In this work we present a novel approach to solving concurrent multiagent planning problems in which several agents act in parallel. Our approach relies on a compilation from concurrent multiagent planning to classical planning, allowing us to use an off-the-shelf classical planner to solve the original multiagent problem. The solution can be directly interpreted as a concurrent plan that satisfies a given set of concurrency constraints, while avoiding the exponential blowup associated with concurrent actions. Theoretically, we show that the compilation is sound and complete. Empirically, we show that our compilation can solve challenging multiagent planning problems that require concurrent actions.
title: Solving Concurrent Multiagent Planning using Classical Planning

summary: In this work, we present new approaches for solving multiagent planning and temporal planning problems. These planning forms are two types of concurrent planning, where actions occur in parallel. The methods we propose rely on a compilation to classical planning problems that can be solved using an off-the-shelf classical planner. Then, the solutions can be converted back into multiagent or temporal solutions. Our compilation for multiagent planning is able to generate concurrent actions that satisfy a set of concurrency constraints. Furthermore, it avoids the exponential blowup associated with concurrent actions, a problem that many multiagent planners are facing nowadays. Incorporating similar ideas in temporal planning enables us to generate temporal plans with simultaneous events, which most state-of-the-art temporal planners cannot do. In experiments, we compare our approaches to other approaches. We show that the methods using transformations to classical planning are able to get better results than state-of-the-art approaches for complex problems. In contrast, we also highlight some of the drawbacks that this kind of methods have for both multiagent and temporal planning. We also illustrate how these methods can be applied to real world domains like the smart mobility domain. In this domain, a group of vehicles and passengers must self-adapt in order to reach their target positions. The adaptation process consists in running a concurrent planning algorithm. The behavior of the approach is then evaluated.
title: Resolution of Concurrent Planning Problems using Classical Planning

content:
This time I’m going to talk about a kind of spin-off of my previous post, where I explained some experiences in the PSP scene. The parallel experience consisted in learning how to use design programs like Adobe Photoshop and GIMP. Here is the explanation of how I started designing signatures in forums!
As soon as I had my PSP hacked, my participation in forums began to increase. I had previously visited some of them but just as a visitor, especially in ESPALPSP as I explained in the first post related to these “adventures”. However, although it might seem strange, I didn’t start to post in the PSP related sections, but in the graphic design one!
The very first thing I remember when entering in the design section was a post about a tournament… but about what? It was about signature design. By that time I had no idea what a signature was. The signature is what always appears when you post something in a forum; users usually put an image instead of text. I was completely determined to participate even though I had never used GIMP or Photoshop.
The topic of the tournament was about a creature of the manga Bleach whose name was Kon. Being highly motivated to achieve something, I did some “research” for tutorials about getting started with this new software. I don’t remember if I spent many days with the design, but I definitely got something, which is shown below. I knew it was simple, the characters were not very well mixed with the background, and there were too many effects applied, but I felt (and I feel) very proud of it.
   You might have wondered what does M14_b2 mean? Well, that was my second name in the Internet after Ertsiger (which I have recently started to use again). Actually, I borrowed it from my mother. She created it by using her initials, ’M’ (name) and ‘B’ (surname), followed by their respective numbers in the alphabet (14 and 2). Sometime later I searched ‘M14’ and ‘b2’ in Google separately, and they turned out to be (unfortunately) a rifle and a bomber!
Returning to the contest… I didn’t won. There were very talented people who made amazing stuff! However, I received some votes and feedback that made me feel extremely happy. I really felt it was a very rewarding experience, so I kept on experimenting and participating in signature designing contests for more than 4 years (always in PSP related forums).
By joining these tournaments at ESPALPSP I started to establish good relationships with some of the members that participated the most. I remember the name of three who I really admired: Ozzblf, Hirakun, and JDesign. I specially have huge appreciation for Ozzblf, he designed me two avatars that I used during all my life in the PSP scene, and also collaborated with me in my first PSP program with graphics (NeoHider, which I mention in the previous post).
 The first one was rounded avatar which had an image of Kakashi Hatake from the manga Naruto; although I had never read or watched Naruto, it was extremely popular at that time (a lot of people had avatars related to it), so I wanted one. I asked him to do this avatar through a post which he used as a personal workshop (some designers used to have one).
 The second one was a squared avatar which contained the text M14_b2 in a very original layout. He told me that it was inspired by cranes that were used to make a new building in front of his house. I loved it, I really appreciated his efforts! Besides, he told me that he was very happy because of trusting him so much, which even increased my respect for him and his work.
     ESPALPSP was not the only place where I participated. Sometime after I started posting at ESPALPSP, another similar website called EstrenosPSP launched. Given my huge interest in designing, I went there looking forward to more challenges. However, the activity there was not very high by that time, so I decided to take charge of the “Graphic Design” section and ask the admins to become a moderator. The person who attracted me to this website (his name was The_ugly and had Eli Wallach as his avatar, although he after changed his name to Wizard_PsP or something similar) knew about my interest and quickly named me moderator.
I felt incredibly happy, so I organized the first weekly tournament of signature design after some time; however, we were not as many people (3 or 4) as in ESPALPSP (usually +20), so it was not as competitive. Unfortunately, the website did not last too much, so I had to visit other websites. Furthermore, I was banned in ESPALPSP because of private messages publicizing EstrenosPSP, i.e. spam (enough good reasons to do so).
I continued designing signatures at two websites: PSPStation.org (now PSNeo.com) and TecMAX.net. The first one is the forum in which I have participated the most (it was mainly about PSP, but now is about everything related to consoles); I stopped posting there in 2010. TecMAX.net was a website that I launched together with three Internet colleagues: GERMAX, The_dark and valen_9. In both forums I used to organize and participate in designing challenges, and I even won my first competitions there. Without a doubt, I consider this time the best as a designer (if I can consider myself as such, since the only thing I usually did was modifying already existing things). I had even received some “orders”, which made me feel very well, especially when people were happy about the results!
   Finally, I want to say that I didn’t only designed signatures and avatars, but also interfaces (for PSP applications, of course)! I did interfaces for my applications and also for the programming team in which I was involved. Apart from these ones, I also did it for other people. Concretely, there was a coder called Recse who made an app named iApplicationX, and he asked me to do the interface for him. As he seemed friendly to me, I accepted (he told me to use a wallpaper he found, and make the design over it).
   And that’s it! I hope you have liked this new post. If you want to start doing this kind of stuff, I highly encourage you to visit DeviantArt. Extremely talented designers post their works there (lots of them done from scratch, which is even more praiseworthy). I used to post some of my work there. In fact, after writing this post, I have noted that lots of things I have done are not there. I believe someday I will make an update. You can check my gallery out by clicking here.
See you soon!
summary:
This time I’m going to talk about a kind of spin-off of my previous post, where I explained some experiences in the PSP scene. The parallel experience consisted in learning how to use design programs like Adobe Photoshop and GIMP. Here is the explanation of how I started designing signatures in forums!
As soon as I had my PSP hacked, my participation in forums began to increase. I had previously visited some of them but just as a visitor, especially in ESPALPSP as I explained in the first post related to these “adventures”.
title: Early Experiences in Graphic Design

content:
In this post I’m going to explain my experiences in the PSP scene. The PSP scene opened many doors for me: meet new people, learn to use design programs, start programming for first time in my life, … Definitely, what I lived from 2007 to 2010 thanks to the PSP community was incredibly helpful for me from then on.
I’ve divided in several chapters the whole content. You can access these chapters from the table of contents below.
Table of contents  Chapter 1: The arrival Chapter 2: The revelation Chapter 3: The day arrived… Chapter 4: Autoinstallers and bans Chapter 5: Translations of plugins Chapter 6: Learning to program the PSP Chapter 7: Joining programming teams Chapter 8: The Scenery Beta 2008 Chapter 9: The Team VGT Chapter 10: The End  Chapter 1: The arrival  After asking several times Santa Claus to gift me a PSP for Christmas, I finally got it. It was 2006. If I remember well, at that time the PSP was one of the most powerful portable consoles together with the NintendoDS (actually, I don’t think there were many more apart from these two “monsters”). The console came in a pack which included the official game of the MotoGP championship (I was and I am a huge fan of motorcycle racing).
For some days I played MotoGP. By that time, I did not have a memory stick that could allowed me to save the game status, so the battery had to be charged and I had to be careful so as not to shutdown the PSP (otherwise, I would loose all my progress). Then, I was bought Grand Theft Auto: Vice City Stories (a really funny game when played with friends via wireless).
Chapter 2: The revelation  It was at mid-January or at the end of January 2007, when I saw the light: all the things that could be done with the console. I do not exactly remember what happened to my computer but it was not working well; thus, we called the technician to see if he could repair it. He came home and after repairing whatever happened to it, he saw my PSP on the table. I remember he asked me something like “Have you hacked it?”; obviously, at that time I knew enough about the computer argot to know he was referring to the execution of videogames (i.e. play a videogame without having acquired it). After answering “No” to his question, he opened his bag and took his PSP; then, he turned it on and… Crash Bandicoot was on the screen! Crash Bandicoot! I spent many hours playing that game during my childhood in the original PlayStation. How did he manage to get it for PSP? The answer: he “freed” the PSP via software. I knew that consoles were hacked via hardware by changing microchips, but at that time I would have never thought that it could be done by software with a permanent effect.
   This revelation really motivated me to achieve the same result. I asked him how did he do it, and he told me “After investigating quite a lot…”. He also told me not to update after observing that my PSP firmware version was 2.81 (he had something strange as version… 3.03 OE). So I decided to “investigate”. That same evening I started to search in Google for ways to free the console. Surprisingly, there were many results… so many that I didn’t know how to start! In some sources the 3.03 version appeared, in others 2.71 SE, and in others 3.10. 2.71 SE was too low, and 3.10 was the newest, so I thought I would install 3.10. I have to recognize that I also felt more intelligent than the technician because I found something newer! Nevertheless, I was mistaken: I had installed an official firmware (of course, I could not run Crash Bandicoot). Precisely, I did what I was told not to do: update the firmware.
After a week, I decided to go to the shop where the technician worked to know if there was something I could do to hack the PSP. However, he just reminded me that he told me not to update… However, he again said me not to update any more until something compatible with my version appeared. Some months later (I think that by May or June, or even before), an exploit that allowed to hack the PSP appeared. It worked for the version 3.50 and required the game Lumines to be used. I was partially lucky: I had version 3.10 but not the game. Thus, the hunt for this game started! But I was not the only one: maybe hundreds of people were in the same situation and the game was discontinued. Thus, I had to fight for one unit in eBay (finally got one for about 30 €, which was not bad at all)!
Chapter 3: The day arrived…  In the end, I could finally hack my PSP! I prepared everything very well since I didn’t want anything to go wrong. By using the exploit of the Lumines game, I got my PSP downgraded to 1.50. Version 1.50 allowed to run homebrew without any kind of additional software (it was the key of all the PSP scene). From that version I could run an updating executable (in PSP all executables were called EBOOT.PBP) that installed a Custom Firmware (CF) in the console.
Another “challenging” decision had to be made. At that time there were two custom firmware families: the CFs made by Dark Alex and the CFs made by the M33 team.
 Dark Alex was a Spanish coder, and probably was the most famous coder of the PSP scene; he released the Special Edition (SE) and Open Edition (OE) firmwares like 2.71 SE, 3.02 OE, 3.03 OE, 3.10 OE, 3.30 OE and 3.40 OE.
 On the other hand, the M33 team was believed to be Russian, and had just released (at that time) the 3.51 M33 and 3.52 M33 firmwares. Dark Alex retired after releasing the 3.40 OE, so the work of the M33 team was the only active one (some months later, it was revealed that Dark Alex was actually part of the M33 team, so he had not retired).
  After thinking a bit about what to do, I decided to install 3.52 M33 in my PSP, and 3.40 OE in my brother’s PSP. Fortunately, everything went quite well.
   From that moment, I started to take advantage that a Custom Firmware had: play PlayStation games, other PSP games, and even I could make the CPU work faster so that games loaded faster! I also started getting into forums, concretely in a Spanish one called ESPALPSP (I believe it closed because of legal issues) which was one of the most crowded forums in Spain.
Chapter 4: Autoinstallers and bans  The forums taught me a lot about what could be done regarding design and PSP. One of the first things that caught my attention was that anybody could make autoinstallers for homebrew and custom firmwares for the console. Afterwards, these installers could be easily shared my uploading them to MegaUpload, RapidShare or MediaFire. Actually, I used one for installing version 3.52 M33 in my PSP (at that time, it was the best option if you did not know how to organize the folders for an installation).
I was so excited that as soon as a new update was released, I made an autoinstaller for it. Sometimes, I shared them with the PSP community to make the life of the other newbies easier.
Nevertheless, I have to say that most of the shared installers (not only mine), tended to crash while copying the files to the PSP. Therefore, almost everybody would finally do the installations manually when they understood what had to be done.
In ESPALPSP forums I met very friendly people. Some nicknames I remember now are Gladiator, The_Ugly, Sarek, Captiva, Amcymonse, Ozzblf or Hirakun. However, one day I was “forced” to leave. I put it in quotation marks because it was completely my fault since I sent some private messages promoting another website called EstrenosPSP.
EstenosPSP was quite recent at the time I spammed. In fact, I was spammed by another ESPALPSP user who turned out to be one the creators of the site (The_Ugly). The main reason I think I did that was because I had a strong longing for power: I wanted to become moderator of a forum to organize it the best I could. In a well-established website like ESPALPSP it was nearly impossible, so I thought: “Why don’t I try in a new one? If they see I start to organize and create content and bring new people, then I can become a moderator”. Thus, I sent some spam messages in ESPALPSP to attract new people, copied my main contributions, and organize design challenges.
ESPALPSP caught me quite quickly and I was banned. Since that moment, I would not return to that forum except for the design competitions (another account was created with the name BlackDemon, I believe).
Although I did my best to make EstrenosPSP successful (with a questionable methodology), the website did not take off. Even the participation of the administrators was extremely low, so the website was doomed to fail.
Chapter 5: Translations of plugins  I looked for a new forum where to participate and I found one called TeknoPSP. This site was much more focused on the PSP scene rather than ESPALPSP and EstrenosPSP, which put special emphasis on publishing games. This last activity never attracted my attention, so TeknoPSP fitted my interests perfectly.
TeknoPSP users mostly published content about programming, modifying existing plugins or customizing the hardware and software of the console. One of the things that I saw that I could do was to translate plugins from English into Spanish. Actually, it was a colleague from ESPALPSP, Gladiator, who suggested me to do it with him.
The plugins were the Recovery LE 3.80 and the Recovery LE 3.90 respectively. They were originally programmed by the Team Wildcard. They allowed to open the recovery menu at any moment in the PSP and to modify its values. It was a quite interesting software because the typical recovery menu could only be opened when powering on the PSP while pressing the R trigger.
The translation was done by editing the hexadecimal code of the program. The literals could be easily found in any HEX editor. Thus, we only had to translate these pieces of text, save the file and share it with th community.
Our work was not 100% accurate, but it was useful to some people (at least, they express gratitude for our contribution). The main reason was that the translated words could not be longer than the original English words; so in some cases, we just left the English words if the translation could not be understood with few characters.
I have looked for some images and I have found one of the translation of the VSH menu originally developed by Red Squirrel. You can see it below.
   I have got this image from a Wordpress blog I created to post my projects. It stayed updated only for a week during June 2008. You can click here to access the blog (I hope it will never disappear).
Chapter 6: Learning to program the PSP  After creating autoinstallers and translating software, I asked myself: “How can these programs be done? Can I do them as well?”.
To answer these questions, I firstly did a quick search on Google and the main result was: “Kit de Programación PSP para Torpes” (in English, it would be something like “PSP Programming Kit for Dummies”). I couldn’t feel dumber at that time, so it was ideal for me to get deeper in the PSP programming world. By the way, the author of the installer (because it was barely more than an installer) was Marce82, who got certain prestige in the scene.
I followed the steps to create the classic “Hello World!” program. Here you have a sample of how it looked like. Obviously, I didn’t understand almost anything. I was a 14 years old teen without programming background (I got my first computer with 10 years and got access to Internet with 13). Anybody in my family or in my group of friends could help me to understand what was going on. To sum up, things were difficult for me.
As you may have seen in the previous link, the programming language was C. Consequently, I can tell that C was the first programming language I learnt. Nonetheless, I must admit that during this period of my programming life, what I usually did was to reuse code from other people to create my applications. I never dominated the language and understood its capabilities fully.
People from the PSP programming world recommended to learn C for computer before starting with PSP. I didn’t do it, but I tried: there was an “academy” in Barcelona which pretended to teach the language you chose. I wanted to learn C, of course, but can you guess what they taught me? Visual Basic. A wasted summer because I didn’t only learn the language I wanted, but also because the quality of the course was extremely poor so I learnt almost nothing.
From what I remember, the LUA programming language was also quite used. I believe that applications written in LUA could be run from another program that called LUA Player. The LUA language was heavily promoted in a community called SceneBeta (originally, betapesepe). In fact, depending on the forum you belonged to, there was a preference for C or LUA. In my case, the preference was for C, so from the beginning I didn’t see anything but C. On the other hand, there were some LUA programmers that started coding in C (e.g. a very talented guy called carlosgs is an example).
One kind of application that was very famous consisted in “hiding” some of the folders of the PSP. From the main menu of the console you could access your music, images or videos from the folders MUSIC, PHOTO or VIDEO respectively. Thus, if you renamed these folders to other names different to the official ones (e.g. XMUSIC, XPHOTO, XVIDEO), the PSP would not show any music, pictures or videos. So what most of these hiding apps did was to rename these folders.
I realised that I also could do this kind of application as soon as I understood how they worked. A proof of this are my two first applications: Ocultador 1.0 and Ocultador 2.0 (the word “ocultador” means “file hide”).
Below there’s an old screenshot I did when I published the second version in my forgotten blog in 2008. A console button was used for either hiding or showing an specific folder (three folders could be treated). Another button allowed the user to exit the application. I think that there wasn’t any message that told whether the folders were being modified, you had to look at the orange flashing LED of the PSP (which indicated that the memory card was accessed).
   Obviously, new software which does the same as existing software without contributing anything original, is doomed to be ignored. And that’s what happened. However, as you will see afterwards, I kept working on it…
Chapter 7: Joining programming teams and new websites Although I was quite proud of my barely original homebrews, I thought that if I joined someone to program with, I would improve much more. One day, I saw in the forums of TeknoPSP post that said something like: “programmer looks for programmers to create a PSP coding team”. It was just what I was looking for!
The nickname of the publisher was GERMAX (his blog is still online). I sent him a private message with my email and saying that I was interested. Some days later or maybe the same day, we met in Windows Live Messenger (feeling old now…). His real name was Germán, he lived in Mexico and was 4 years older than me. He immediately seemed nice to me, and I think it was reciprocal.
By the time we talked, he had already formed a team with two guys called valen_9 and Viorel. The team was named Team VGV because of their initials. They were already preparing some releases, including one called Pixelizador (by that time it was the second version). This application helped to repair dead or stuck pixels by displaying images of different colours at different speed rates. I have just seen that there are videos in YouTube that also do this process!
Small parenthesis: applications that repaired pixels were almost as common as the ones that hid files, so before knowing theirs, I had already used one called UltraScreen done by Marce82 and TheSixthHalcon (nevertheless, I think that the first version of the Pixelizador was older than UltraScreen). Then, I thought it was not too difficult for me to program it, so I did an application which was never released called NeoPixel. From what I remember, my code was horrible: the task could be easily done with loops, but instead I put an instruction for showing each colour one after another… End of the parenthesis.
In the moment I joined the team, Viorel was fired because he never collaborated, and another guy “substituted” him: The_dark. He and valen_9, who were actually called Jorge and Valentín, knew each other since they were from the same town: Badajoz (Spain). The team was renamed to Team VGT.
My relationship with them was excellent during the three years we worked together. We even started a pair of forums called TecPro and TecMax. Both were initially free hosted in 4sql.net, and they used the Icy Phoenix system. The content we published was, as their name state, related to technology (although most of it was focused on games). Everything was fully written in Spanish.
TecMax was created to substitute TecPro (I don’t remember why), and lasted for some months. We got more than 1000 users there, and then we though that we wanted to leave the free hosting to have something more professional: we founded TecMax.net.
Some time before launching TecMax, the team and me joined another website called PSPStation.org. This site was created by a group of people from TeknoPSP that was not satisfied with the way the page was directed. My colleague Gladiator told me to sign in and I did (I think I was the seventh registered user or so).
The environment at PSPStation was incredibly friendly and I learnt a lot of new things. Besides, I met new people with whom I talked many times through Messenger. Some of the nicknames I remember now are Adrin, Raisen, Zheive, Zaqui, Noninane, Aw, … Most of the times I designed signatures for them or interfaces for their homebrews. Actually, after the fiasco of EstrenosPSP, I started organizing some of the design challenges there. I even recorded some videos for their YouTube channel:
 Design tutorials (1, 2, 3). Gameplays (1). PSP tutorials (1, 2, 3). Showing homebrew (1, 2, 3, 4).  Probably, I haven’t been happier than in PSPStation and TecMax. Having said this… I wouldn’t participate in the creation of homebrew with the Team VGT until after taking part in my first PSP programming contest: the Scenery Beta 2008.
Chapter 8: The Scenery Beta 2008 In 2008, one of the main references in the Spanish scene, SceneBeta, organizes an international programming contest: the Scenery Beta International Homebrew Showcase (article, rules).
   It consisted of different categories, one for each different platform: PSP, NintendoDS, PC and Wii. For each category, there were two subcategories: games and apps. The five most voted creations for each category received a price (from a trophy and money for the winner, to a pen drive for the last one). The votes were a combination between the users and participants votes.
From the very beginning, I was determined to participate even with an application that hid files… which was what finally happened. Ocultador 2.0 had not a graphical interface, so I decided that the main novelty would be to add one. I knew I wasn’t going to succeed since it was still not original, but my technical capabilities were poor. In hindsight, I missed lots of the fundamentals of C (and, in general, of programming) that were required to do something with some aspirations.
I programmed the application with the help of my new friend Germán. The design was done by Ozzblf, who I met in the design subforum of ESPALPSP; I admired him a lot because his designs always looked awesome to me. However, I believe that I pushed him so hard to have the interface designed quickly, that he desisted to do his best (concretely, in the screen where the folders to hide appear, the other ones are astonishing). It was completely my fault because: (1) there was enough time to substitute the prototype images for the good ones (1 minute), (2) he was doing it without expecting anything in return. This is something I’ve tried to correct in the last years: don’t put unnecessary pressure on a person that you know he/she is doing everything he/she can.
By the way, the final application was called NeoHider v1.0. I used the word “hider” as a translation of “ocultador”, but it seems it isn’t officially used anywhere. I remember having checked if it was correct, but I suppose Google set up a trap for me.
                         On the other hand, the Team VGT published the fourth version of the Pixelizador, which had better graphics and usability respect to previous versions. Although there were some applications of the same kind, their proposal was one of the first, so they were recognized by its originality. I recorded a video for them showing how the application worked, and it’s still in YouTube.
  Many people at SceneBeta criticised me for presenting NeoHider to the contest. Their argument was that it wasn’t original enough. I knew that, but I tried to do my best and, as I’ve previously said, my capabilities were not incredible. In contrast, I received a lot of support from people of PSPStation to whom I’ll always be grateful. Without them, the Team VGT and Ozzblf the application couldn’t have been made.
Finally, I qualified 7th out of 9 participants by user votes of my category, PSP apps (user votes, final results). My colleagues in Team VGT did well enough to finish 5th (they won a customized pen drive). Another friend, Adrin, finished 3rd with an app called Project0; it was an app that included multiple tools to manage the PSP, including one to hide folders of the PSP.
Chapter 9: The Team VGT  After the Scenery Beta 2008, I started programming with the Team VGT. We weren’t really productive in terms of released software, but we made some interesting tools.
One of them was MaxFlasher v1.0. It was a software that included the following functionalities: backup of the four different flashes of the PSP, free useless space of the console, and hide/show folders. Besides, it was the first application we did that allowed choosing between English and Spanish.
The interface of MaxFlasher was done using the VLF library, which allowed to do applications with the same appearance than PSP official apps. At the time we published it, using VLF was a huge trend.
   In 2009, SceneBeta was organizing again the Scenery Beta contest. We were going to participate and this time I would count as a member of the team. We planned to do another application which had multiple tools that could be used. Its final name was VGT Evolution. It was even published in QJ.net, a very prestigious site in the PSP scene. The video below shows what could be done with the application.
  We were very proud of what we achieved. We felt that we could finish in the top-5, but we didn’t (results). From my point of view, there was a controversy that affected us very negatively. The rules clearly stated that the software which used some element under copyright wouldn’t be accepted. Nevertheless, there was one application which took many elements (icons, wallpapers, sounds) of Windows Vista; in fact, its name was Cristal Vista.
When designing the interface of VGT Evolution, we always chose icons with free licenses. Then, Cristal Vista appears and it’s accepted. So we agreed that it was not fair that it could compete against us and other applications which proposed designs that made sure to avoid copyright problems.
The main problem was that one of us decided to complain in public. A better idea, from my point of view, would have been to tell to the organizers privately. I suppose we do it privately as well, but the public complain was totally out of place.
The organizers understood that the app shouldn’t have been accepted, so they disqualified the author. The author of Cristal Vista had the opportunity to change the interface and apply again. He did and presented the same application with a different design: Cristal Sky. I couldn’t agree more with the organizers that it was the right way to act.
When the new of disqualification was published (source), the author announced that he may quit the scene. He received a lot of support from everybody, while we were insulted many times. If that public message hadn’t been published, we wouldn’t have received that bad press. I consider that we lost many votes because of this.
VGT Evolution finished the 11th out of 17 apps (results). We didn’t expect that results when we presented the app to the contest, so it was a bit discouraging. From that moment, I started to lose contact with my friends in Team VGT: VGT Evolution would be the last app we released.
Chapter 10: The End  Since the end of the Scenery Beta 2009, I started to leave the PSP and, in general, everything related to the PSP.
One of the causes was that I started to require more time for studying, but I also lost motivation. The same happened to my colleagues in Team VGT. For example, we stopped participating in our own website, TecMax. Since we didn’t want to close it, we substituted the forum and set up a blog instead.
We thought that the blog wouldn’t require so much time, but we were wrong. Thus, we eventually stopped posting content by the end of 2010 and we closed the website.
Something similar occurred with my participation in PSPStation, where I was in charge of organizing the design contests. Some weeks I forgot to open the contest, so I decided that more active people should do it.
Finally, I tried to do one last homebrew for the Scenery Beta 2010. This homebrew was named GuitarTab. At that time, I started to try playing the electric guitar, so I thought that it would be useful for other people and me, to have an app that could show tabs in a light device like the PSP.
The app didn’t enter the contest because it had important bugs loading the tabs. Besides, it wasn’t easily maintainable. The reason was that the tabs were images, so creating a tab consisted in creating many images. I created some and I have to recognize it was a complete nightmare… Maybe it would have been easier to load text files, and render them in some appropriate way.
Well, this is the end of my memories of the PSP scene. I hope you’ve enjoyed reading it!
summary:
In this post I’m going to explain my experiences in the PSP scene. The PSP scene opened many doors for me: meet new people, learn to use design programs, start programming for first time in my life, … Definitely, what I lived from 2007 to 2010 thanks to the PSP community was incredibly helpful for me from then on.
I’ve divided in several chapters the whole content. You can access these chapters from the table of contents below.
title: Adventures in the PSP Scene

content:
I have decided to write my first real post. I’m going to briefly write about a bunch of projects related to Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) using Arduino boards and XBee modules.
During my 3rd year in the Computer Science degree (2013 - 2014), I coursed a subject called Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN). My teammates, Sergio Barrachina Muñoz and Francesc Wilhelmi Roca, and me did some projects during this course.
During the course, we used an Arduino board (which now outside USA is called Genuino) together with some electronic components (e.g. LED lights or servomotors). The usage of these elements varied depending on the project we did; nevertheless, for the projects involving wireless communication (which was, in the end, the purpose of the course) every team used a pair of XBee boards.
   The course followed a pretty different structure from what the students of Pompeu Fabra University are used to. In fact, not only the students, but also people from outside the university could follow the course. To do so, an online course was created at Peer to Peer University (P2PU) called A Hands-on Experience with Wireless Sensor Networks. A website was created to access the PDF course guide and the P2PU course itself: handsonwsn.org.
Each team created a blog in which the members posted their projects (e.g. description, wiring diagrams, videos, …). Our blog (still online) was Getting Into Arduino; there we posted a quite detailed description of everything we did for the lab (including the code which I uploaded some weeks ago to GitHub so as not to loose it). We did not reference WSN in the title name because at that time we did not come up with anything better (and now it is too late to do the change).
Now, I give a brief description of the projects we did and the link to the corresponding post in the blog:
 Traffic Lights: Implemented a traffic light system. It did not involve wireless communication, it was a project to start getting familiarized with Arduino and its electronics.
 Chatting with XBee: An XBee connected to a PC sent messages written by the user to another XBee connected to the Arduino. An attached LCD screen displayed the received messages.
 Melody Selector using XBee: An Arduino + XBee system is used to select a melody number (displayed in an LCD screen). The number is sent to another Arduino + XBee system that will play the melody using a buzzer.
 Light Based Alarm: A buzzer sounds when the amount of light received by a photoresistor is not enough.
 Morse Code Player: An XBee sends messages to another XBee connected to the Arduino. These messages are translated to Morse code and played using a buzzer.
 Activating a Fan Depending on the Temperature: An XBee connected to an Arduino detects the temperature of a room; then, this temperature information is sent to another XBee connected to a PC which determines if a fan should be activated. If the temperature is too high, then the second XBee tells the first one to enable the fan so as to reduce the room’s temperature.
 Measuring Light Pollution in Barcelona: Light readings made by a photoresistor are periodically uploaded (as JSON) to Xively.
 Remotely Controlled Car: A control pad with an Arduino and an XBee sends commands to a battery powered car. The car is formed by a pair of motors, and an XBee that receives the commands and powers the motors accordingly.
  Just as an interesting project that is done internationally, I wanted to comment the following. During the course, the class visited the Fab Lab Barcelona, where we were explained the Smart Citizen project. As their website says: “Smart Citizen is a platform to generate participatory processes of people in the cities. Connecting data, people and knowledge, the objective of the platform is to serve as a node for building productive and open indicators, and distributed tools, and thereafter the collective construction of the city for its own inhabitants.”. The following video is much more illustrative than the explanation:
  And that’s it! By now, it’s my first post. Let me know your thoughts in the comments.
summary:

During my 3rd year in the Computer Science degree (2013 - 2014), I coursed a subject called Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN). My teammates, Sergio Barrachina Muñoz and Francesc Wilhelmi Roca, and me did some projects during this course.

title: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) using Arduino and XBee

content:
Hi everyone! After some months (and even years) thinking about starting my own webpage, I have finally done it. The main reason for which I did not do it before was that I thought I could not explain anything interesting… and maybe I cannot! However, I wanted to have a place where I could explain experiences, as well as things I have learnt, things I am learning, and things I will (hopefully) learn.
Last year, in July 2015, I graduated in Computer Science from Pompeu Fabra University (Barcelona). So basically here I will talk about topics related to Computer Science. However, I have many hobbies about which I do not know whether I will post about them or not (probably, I could talk about them from a computational perspective). At this moment, I am planning to post projects that I did during my bachelor’s degree years, as well as some pre-degree experiments that I consider interesting to talk about (e.g. how I started programming software).
Unfortunately, I started to use GitHub and Bitbucket short time ago, so many code I have written during these last years is not there. Nevertheless, I think I will upload some content there although I will not do any more commits… probably it could be eventually useful for somebody.
I will try to post as usually as possible (1 or 2 post per week). But I have to say that I do not guarantee that this will happen.
Anyway, welcome to my blog! I hope that you will enjoy it!
summary: Hi everyone! After some months (and even years) thinking about starting my own webpage, I have finally done it. The main reason for which I did not do it before was that I thought I could not explain anything interesting… and maybe I cannot! However, I wanted to have a place where I could explain experiences, as well as things I have learnt, things I am learning, and things I will (hopefully) learn.
title: Welcome to my blog!

summary: This thesis describes the design of agents that learn to play Atari games using the Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) framework to interact with them. The application of machine learning in video games, given its high complexity, is considered to be a bridge towards real-world domains such as robotics. The goal in Atari games is to achieve the highest possible score. To solve this task, reinforcement learning and search techniques are used. These algorithms outperform humans in 30 of the 61 games supported by ALE. Since humans are very good at making generalizations between games, special emphasis is/ngiven to evaluating how well an agent learns from multiple games simultaneously. These experiments usually result in a higher score for specific pairs of games. Besides, there are games that tend to increase their score when playing with other games, whereas there are games that help others to perform better.
title: Learning and Generalization in Atari Games
